teamObjective.explanation: With my partner publicly near finish (only two cards left), I want to make the trick as easy as possible for them to take, preserving their stronger cards and my own slightly higher diamonds for later.

partnerBelief.summary: My partner likely has very few cards left and may be trying to finish this round, based on the public hand count and the partner_near_finish tag.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents have not revealed any cards, so their exact holdings are uncertain; they may have higher non-trump singles or trumps that could beat a low diamond lead.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the 3♦, my lowest card, gives my near-finish partner the best chance to win the trick while preserving my 4♦-6♦ as safer exit cards. It also avoids wasting any meager strength I have on a lead that is unlikely to stand.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: Playing the 6♦ uses up my highest non-trump card for a lead that opponents can almost certainly beat, leaving me with even weaker follow-ups.
The 5♦ is still a low, easily beaten card and does not meaningfully increase the chance of winning the trick; keeping it preserves mid-ranked options.
The 4♦ is only marginally stronger than the 3♦ and offers no strategic advantage, whereas the 3♦ is the safest lead for supporting my partner.

risks: An opponent may have a higher non-trump single or any trump and take control of the trick.
If my partner cannot beat the 3♦, opponents may gain tempo and delay our team's finish.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By leading the absolute lowest card, I minimize resource loss and leave my partner the widest range of possible responses to win the trick and finish.

notes: Opening lead with a very weak hand; priority is partner support rather than trick strength.
